result:
I’ve been thinking about the way certain smells are time machines. The other afternoon I opened a box of cedar pencils I hadn’t touched since college, and suddenly I was twenty again, sitting on a cracked fire-escape in Richmond, Virginia, convinced that every word I might write could change the gravitational constant of the world. The graphite smelled like thunderstorms, and the city below smelled like river-silt and frying cumin from the kebab trucks. I remember believing that if I just kept the pencil moving, the future would stay soft, like wax I could still press my thumb into and reshape.

Now the pencils are shorter, their cedar skins tattooed with tooth-marks from a puppy I owned and lost in the year my father forgot my birthday. I don’t write with them anymore; I keep them like relics, the way medieval households sheltered splinters of saints’ bones. Touching them is a form of quiet apostasy: I no longer believe the world is pliable, but I keep the evidence that I once did.

Entropy has become the quiet roommate who rearranges the furniture overnight. I find coasters in the freezer, the left shoe of every pair inexplicably more worn than the right, and my memories sorting themselves into smaller boxes labeled with increasingly vague adjectives: “warm,” “loud,” “almost.” I used to fear this reorganization, as if forgetting the precise shade of my mother’s nail polish meant I was letting her die twice. But I’m learning that forgetting is its own mercy—an emotional agriculture that lets last year’s grief lie fallow so something new can elbow up through the crust.

Last week I watched a time-lapse video of moss reclaims a discarded children’s shoe in a forest. Over two minutes, the velvet green swallows the polyester red, turning the lace into a vein, the toe into a soft hillside. I felt an uncomplicated happiness I hadn’t earned, the way you feel lighter when someone else loses weight. It reminded me that reclamation is the planet’s oldest love story: matter insisting on returning to the communal body, on being re-inked into the great palimpsest. We call it decay because we’re vain enough to think possession is the default state of things.

I keep a list of small, impossible wishes: (1) to step on a shadow and hear it sigh, (2) to mispronounce my own name so charmingly that language itself bends to accommodate me, (3) to receive a postcard from a dream, postmarked with tomorrow’s date. None of them will happen, but the ache they leave is pleasant, like pressing on a bruise to verify the miracle of healing. The wishes remind me that I’m still porous, still tunneling toward something, even if the map is drawn on freezer paper that smudges at the first hint of steam.

Tonight the moon looks like a chipped dinner plate someone rinsed and set on the drainboard of the sky. I stand outside in bare feet, testing the wind with my tongue the way kids test batteries with their tongues—checking for a spark, for proof that the atmosphere still remembers how to complete a circuit. Somewhere a neighbor’s sprinkler goes ts-ts-ts, baptizing the sidewalk in cheap, chlorinated grace. I think about how water never asks permission to change form, how it simply rises or falls or hardens, marrying whatever temperature is offered. I’d like to be that obedient to transition, to refuse the luxury of nostalgia and simply enter the next state with my hands unclenched.

But I’m not there yet. I’m still the sum of every smell, every pencil shaving, every shoe swallowed by moss. I’m the echo and the original cry. I’m the time-lapse and the single frame. I’m the bruise and the thumb that presses it, testing for pain, testing for the miracle of being tender enough to still feel.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
